title: break, continue and return

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for break, continue and return; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: GNU C Language Introduction and Reference Manual; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to break, continue and return
sources.claim: break exits the nearest loop or switch, continue advances the nearest loop to its next iteration step, return leaves a function, and goto transfers within one function.

canonicalExplanation.summary: break exits the nearest loop or switch, continue advances the nearest loop to its next iteration step, return leaves a function, and goto transfers within one function.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A nested search uses a flag or helper return because break exits only the inner loop rather than both loop levels. Your goal is to refactor loop and function exits for correct behavior and readable control flow. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Operators and precedence; pf control iteration; while, do and for. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: break exits the nearest loop or switch, continue advances the nearest loop to its next iteration step, return leaves a function, and goto transfers within one function. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Transfers can skip initialization or cleanup logic, nested constructs change the nearest target, and goto cannot enter scopes incompatibly with variably modified objects. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: break exits the current function and continue restarts the complete program from its first statement.

includes: Use structured early exits without obscuring invariants or cleanup.
Refactor loop and function exits for correct behavior and readable control flow.
excludes: Transfers can skip initialization or cleanup logic, nested constructs change the nearest target, and goto cannot enter scopes incompatibly with variably modified objects.

outcomes: Refactor loop and function exits for correct behavior and readable control flow.

examples.title: break, continue and return: defined case
examples.situation: A nested search uses a flag or helper return because break exits only the inner loop rather than both loop levels.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A nested search uses a flag or helper return because break exits only the inner loop rather than both loop levels.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: break targets the nearest loop or switch, continue targets the nearest loop, and return is the transfer that exits the current function.
examples.result: The defined result follows because break targets the nearest loop or switch, continue targets the nearest loop, and return is the transfer that exits the current function.

examples.title: break, continue and return: boundary case
examples.situation: A single cleanup label releases resources acquired along several failure paths before the function returns one status.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A single cleanup label releases resources acquired along several failure paths before the function returns one status.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Transfers can skip initialization or cleanup logic, nested constructs change the nearest target, and goto cannot enter scopes incompatibly with variably modified objects.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: break exits the current function and continue restarts the complete program from its first statement.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Refactor loop and function exits for correct behavior and readable control flow.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model break, continue and return: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
